---
title: "Activity 08: Hiearchal Clustering"
author: "Navarroza"
date: "June 11"
output: html_document
---
  
```{r setup}
setwd("C:/Users/Admin/downloads")
library(cluster)
morph_data <- read.csv("morphology_data.csv")
morph_clean <- morph_data[, !(names(morph_data) %in% c("Pop", "Sex"))]
morph_clean <- na.omit(morph_clean)
dist_mat <- dist(scale(morph_clean), method = "euclidean")
k_range <- 2:10
sil_ward <- numeric(length(k_range))
sil_single <- numeric(length(k_range))
for (i in seq_along(k_range)) {
  k <- k_range[i]
  
  # Ward's Method
  h_ward <- hclust(dist_mat, method = "ward.D2")
  sil_ward[i] <- mean(silhouette(cutree(h_ward, k), dist_mat)[, 3])
  # Single Linkage
  h_single <- hclust(dist_mat, method = "single")
  sil_single[i] <- mean(silhouette(cutree(h_single, k), dist_mat)[, 3])
}

plot(k_range, sil_ward, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "blue", 
     ylim = c(min(c(sil_ward, sil_single)), max(c(sil_ward, sil_single))),
     xlab = "Number of clusters (K)", 
     ylab = "Average Silhouette Width",
     main = "Silhouette Scores: Ward vs. Single Linkage")

lines(k_range, sil_single, type = "b", pch = 19, col = "red")
legend("topright", legend = c("Ward's", "Single Linkage"), 
       col = c("blue", "red"), lty = 1, pch = 19)
result<-PCAtest(frogs_scaled, 100, 100, 0.05, varcorr=FALSE, counter=FALSE, plot=TRUE)
print(result)



hclust_ward <- hclust(dist_mat, method = "ward.D2")
hclust_single <- hclust(dist_mat, method = "single")
plot(hclust_ward, main = "Dendrogram: Ward D2", sub = "", xlab = "Lizard Index",ylab = "Height")
rect.hclust(hclust_ward, k = 3, border = c("red","blue","green"))
plot(hclust_single, main = "Dendrogram: Single Linkage", xlab = "Lizard Index", sub = "", ylab = "Height")
rect.hclust(hclust_single, k = 3, border = c("red","blue","green"))

#B.
#Are the clusters consistent, and what causes the differences?
#K-means and hierarchical clustering often produce different results because they use completely different mathematical rules to group the data. K-means forces data points into specific groups around a central average, while hierarchical clustering builds a tree-like structure by connecting the most similar points one by one. Because of these different approaches, There is adifference in the specific lizards assigned to each cluster change depending on which algorithm you choose.

#How does this influence your interpretation of biological patterns?
#When the clusters are inconsistent, it suggests that the lizards may not actually belong to perfectly defined or separate groups. This tells us that their physical traits might exist on a continuous range rather than splitting into distinct biological types. Relying on only one method can be misleading, so comparing the results helps us realize that the biological patterns are more complex than a simple lumping and splitting classification.
```
